planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before transplant; in zone 9 start December–January and transplant February–March. Green Thai Eggplant is exceptionally heat-tolerant and continues producing through zone 9 summer heat when other eggplant varieties slow. Multiple plantings (February and July) provide nearly year-round production in zone 9. Plants may survive mild winters in zone 9b as short-lived perennials and re-grow from the base.

directions: Transplant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun with warm soil temperatures; Green Thai Eggplant produces small, round to oval, golf-ball-to-tennis-ball-sized fruits in bright green to greenish-white with striping or mottling, harvested young at marble to golf ball size for the best flavor and texture. The flesh is firmer, drier, and significantly more bitter than standard globe eggplant — the characteristic bitterness and firm texture are intentional qualities in Thai and Southeast Asian cooking, where the small green eggplants are sliced and added raw to green curry pastes or braised whole in red curry. They absorb coconut milk and spice sauces without becoming mushy, maintaining their distinctive firm bite even in extended cooking. Unlike the standard Italian or Japanese eggplants where bitterness is considered a defect, the natural bitterness of green Thai eggplant is a flavor feature that rounds and deepens the overall taste of Thai curries. In zone 9 the plants are extremely heat-tolerant and productive through the hottest months.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Eggplant Varieties for Florida
Kitazawa Seed Company — Thai Eggplant Growing Notes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Specialty Eggplant for Southeast Texas

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplant; in zone 9 start February–March and transplant March–April. Fino Verde tolerates more heat than large-leafed basil types and can continue producing through early summer in zone 9 before heat finally causes bolting. In zone 9 start a second planting in July–August for fall production. Pinch flower buds regularly to extend the productive leaf season.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun; Fino Verde is the classic Italian Greek-style dwarf basil used throughout southern Italy as a border herb, pot herb, and fresh seasoning for Caprese and Neapolitan-style dishes — producing a dense, compact mound of tiny, intensely fragrant leaves with a strong, clove-spicy basil aroma more concentrated than large-leafed sweet basil varieties. The extremely small, oval leaves (roughly the size of a fingernail) are traditionally used whole, directly stripped from the stem, and scattered over sliced tomatoes and mozzarella, into pasta, or onto pizza immediately before serving — the tiny leaf size means no chopping is needed. Fino Verde is the correct basil for traditional Neapolitan pizza making, where the small leaves are placed on the cooked pizza whole. The naturally compact, self-branching mound habit needs minimal pinching and forms a tidy, ornamental rounded shape in containers or along garden borders. The flavor intensity is noticeably higher per leaf than in large-leafed sweet basil.
sources: Seeds of Italy — Fino Verde Basil Growing Notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Basil Cultivar Comparison
Richters Herb Catalogue — Italian Dwarf Basil Varieties

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplant; in zone 9 start February–March and transplant March–April. Queen of Siam is more bolt-resistant than many sweet basil varieties and performs well through zone 9 summer heat. A second planting in July–August provides fall production. Pinch flower buds as they form to extend the leaf harvest.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun; Queen of Siam basil is an ornamental and culinary hybrid producing striking dark purple-stemmed plants with large, green leaves and purple-flushed undersides, topped by spectacular spikes of deep purple to magenta flower bracts — one of the most ornamentally dramatic of all basil types. The flavor combines the clove-sweet base of sweet basil with a distinct anise and licorice note similar to Thai basil, making it suitable for both Italian and Southeast Asian cooking. The bolt-resistant, continuously flowering habit produces an extended season of ornamental bloom that makes this basil equally valuable in the flower garden as in the herb garden. The deep purple flower spikes are excellent as cut flowers. In zone 9 the heat tolerance and continuous flower production through summer make Queen of Siam the most reliably ornamental basil for summer landscapes, where it continues flowering attractively even after the flavor of the leaves has declined with heat.
sources: Richters Herb Catalogue — Queen of Siam Basil
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ornamental Herbs for Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Basil Varieties

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall or early spring; in zone 9 plant September–November for best establishment during the cool rainy season. Toyon is drought-tolerant once established and should be watered minimally after the first year — summer irrigation encourages root disease. It is adapted to California's Mediterranean climate with dry summers and winter rainfall; the Gulf Coast's year-round rainfall requires excellent drainage and minimal watering once established.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in extremely well-drained, lean soil — avoid summer irrigation once established; Toyon (California Holly, Christmas Berry) is a large, handsome, evergreen shrub native to California chaparral and oak woodland, producing spectacular clusters of brilliant red to orange-red berries in November–January — the traditional holiday decoration plant of coastal California and the plant thought to have inspired the name 'Hollywood.' The berries have a slightly bitter, astringent flavor when raw but become edible and pleasant when fully ripe and soft after frost or light cooking; indigenous Californians ate them roasted, fermented into cider, or dried. The white flower clusters in summer attract bees, and the winter berries support mockingbirds, waxwings, and other fruit-eating birds through the season when few other fruiting plants are active. Toyon is one of the most ornamentally rewarding California natives for gardens in the dry western zones and Mediterranean climates; its tolerance of the Gulf Coast requires excellent drainage and restrained irrigation.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Heteromeles arbutifolia
University of California Cooperative Extension — California Native Shrubs
Las Pilitas Nursery — Toyon for California Landscapes

planting.note: Direct sow in fall; in zone 9 sow September–November for harvest November–February. Rouge Grenobloise is exceptionally cold-hardy for a butterhead type, surviving temperatures to 15–20°F, which makes it the standard butterhead for fall-through-winter production in zone 9. A spring planting in January–February provides a second harvest before heat causes bolting. The deep red color intensifies in cold weather — fall and winter heads are the most deeply colored.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Rouge Grenobloise is a French heirloom butterhead lettuce from the Grenoble region of southeastern France, selected for exceptional cold-hardiness in the alpine climate of the French Alps — its ability to withstand temperatures to 15–20°F makes it one of the most cold-tolerant butterhead lettuces available. The large, loose heads have deeply red-bronzed outer leaves with green-to-yellow-green interior leaves, creating a bicolor head that is visually striking at harvest. The flavor is the classic buttery, tender, mild butterhead type with a slight sweetness that intensifies with cold exposure — the deep burgundy-red outer leaves have a richer, slightly more complex flavor than the green interior. In zone 9 Rouge Grenobloise provides the most reliable fall-to-winter butterhead harvest of any lettuce variety, forming substantial heads from October plantings that are ready from November through February. Harvest whole heads or strip outer leaves for a continuing harvest.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Rouge Grenobloise Lettuce Variety Notes
Seeds of Italy — French Heirloom Lettuce Varieties
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Cold-Hardy Lettuce Types
